coredata.dc:title: Efficient green light-based photocatalytic removal of bilirubin by a biocompatible nanocomposite of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots/Au
coredata.dc:creator: Dejpasand, Mohamad Taghi
Ardekani, Saeed Rahemi
Ardekani, Asma Sakhaei
Saievar-Iranizad, Esmaiel
Soleymani, Farzad
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/Au (NGQDs/Au) nanocomposite was synthesized by hydrothermal method followed by a photochemical route. The gram-scale nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot was synthesized by using hydrothermal method from urea and citric acid as a precursor. By adding HAuCl4 to the prepared sample, the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/Au nanocomposite was made in a straightforward photochemical process. The synthesized composite was utilized directly as a biocompatible photocatalyst for bilirubin degradation under green light irradiation. The nanocomposite was characterized using FESEM, Raman, XRD, TEM, UV–Vis spectroscopy, and PL. The quantum yield of the nanocomposite was calculated. The role of surface plasmon resonance (SPR) of Au nanoparticles in reduction of the quantum yield and quantum yield effect on the photodegradation process was investigated. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of bilirubin in presence of the synthesized nanocomposite was measured to be nearly 74.7 % after 100 min green light irradiation, which is 5 times more than blue light used in clinical phototherapy without any photocatalyst. The optical analysis results reveal that the doping of nitrogen in GQDs and compositing with gold could accelerate the suppression of charge carrier's recombination. The future application of biocompatible photocatalysts in jaundice treatment under the green light irradiation was also approved in this research.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Photocatalytic
Bilirubin
Green light
Nano composite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dot synthesis
Possible mechanism for photodegradation of bilirubin under green light irradiation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank Research Council of Tarbiat Modares University for financial supports.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the synthesis of NGQD/Au nanocomposite.
TEM image (a) and FESEM image (b) of NGQDs/Au nanoparticles.
X-ray diffraction pattern (a), Raman spectrum (b) and EDX analysis (c) of the NGQDs/Au nanoparticles.
Photoluminescence spectrum of NGQDs and NGQDs/Au nanocomposite at the excitation wavelength of a) 380 nm and b) 460 nm.
UV–Vis spectra of bilirubin solution in the presence of NGQDs/Au nanocomposite under green light irradiation.
Degradation percent of bilirubin solution in the dark mode and green light irradiation.
Schematic illustration of photocatalytic mechanism of NGQDs/Au for photodegradation of bilirubin under green light irradiation.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic performance of NGQDs/Au nanocomposite in photodegradation of bilirubin under irradiation of green light.

head.ce:title: Efficient green light-based photocatalytic removal of bilirubin by a biocompatible nanocomposite of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots/Au

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors have the same contribution in this paper.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/Au (NGQDs/Au) nanocomposite was synthesized by hydrothermal method followed by a photochemical route. The gram-scale nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot was synthesized by using hydrothermal method from urea and citric acid as a precursor. By adding HAuCl4 to the prepared sample, the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/Au nanocomposite was made in a straightforward photochemical process. The synthesized composite was utilized directly as a biocompatible photocatalyst for bilirubin degradation under green light irradiation.

The nanocomposite was characterized using FESEM, Raman, XRD, TEM, UV–Vis spectroscopy, and PL. The quantum yield of the nanocomposite was calculated. The role of surface plasmon resonance (SPR) of Au nanoparticles in reduction of the quantum yield and quantum yield effect on the photodegradation process was investigated. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of bilirubin in presence of the synthesized nanocomposite was measured to be nearly 74.7 % after 100 min green light irradiation, which is 5 times more than blue light used in clinical phototherapy without any photocatalyst. The optical analysis results reveal that the doping of nitrogen in GQDs and compositing with gold could accelerate the suppression of charge carrier's recombination. The future application of biocompatible photocatalysts in jaundice treatment under the green light irradiation was also approved in this research.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the applications of carbon nanostructures in numerous fields such as energy, environment, aerospace, and medicine have significantly increased [1–5]. Good biocompatibility, high chemical and physical stability, and low toxicity are the main reasons for this widespread popularity of carbon nanostructures materials [6–9]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are one of the most widely used carbon nanostructures, which, in addition to the above-mentioned features, have unique electronic and optical properties such as a variable band gap (0–6 electron volts) and the ability to optimize the amount of absorption and emission of photons. Therefore, they have also become an attractive option for various applications such as solar cells, detectors, supercapacitors, biosensors, drug delivery, bioimaging, phototherapy, and photocatalytic technologies [10–14]. The optical properties of GQDs may be improved for use in a specific application by various methods, including changing the size, band edges, functional groups, and doping elements or formation of a composite with other structures [15–19].

Photocatalytic degradation process under visible light irradiation has been of great interest in recent years, both in the research and industrial phases [20–23]. In medical applications, choosing a radiation wavelength range that minimizes the risk is very important. For instance, in the phototherapy treatment for neonatal jaundice, blue light is currently used for converting the water insoluble ZZ-bilirubin isomer into water-soluble ZE-bilirubin isomer before it can accumulate and cause permanent brain damage or death. Although blue light phototherapy is successful in most cases, but due to the presence of ultraviolet light, it is seriously harmful to the skin and eyes of humans and do damages to cellular DNA [24]. Finding a solution to use less energy rays and increase the rate of bilirubin molecules degradation can reduce the possible risks of the mentioned treatment for babies. Compared to blue light irradiation, the irradiation of green light (490–570 nm) is moderate and more biotolerable, but it generally has not been useful because they fall outside the bilirubin absorption band [25]. In this respect, biocompatible visible light-active (VLA) photocatalysts can solve the problem for bilirubin phototherapy [26,27]. For this purpose, many articles have been published in recent years that are looking for a photocatalyst to break down bilirubin molecules with lower energy in radiation and in less time.

Herein, to design an ideal photocatalyst for bilirubin phototherapy and giving consideration to future application in vivo, we have focused on the synthesis of nitrogen doped GQDs/Au (NGQDs/Au) for efficient photodegradation of bilirubin under irradiation of green light. Photocatalytic removal of bilirubin over the biocompatible nanocomposite of NGQDs/Au was performed under a green light source. The prepared nanocomposite exhibits efficient photocatalytic performance in removal of bilirubin.
All chemical reagents and materials were purchased from Merck and utilized without any purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dot synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: NGQDs were synthesized by using hydrothermal route. In the synthesis process, citric acid, urea and deionized water have been used as precursors. Citric acid provides carbon atoms and urea plays a role as a nitrogen atom supplier. The synthesis process, optical and structural characterization, and the calculation of the quantum efficiency have been explained in our previous work [28].

In this step, a nanocomposite of NGQDs and gold nanoparticles is formed by a photochemical process in presence of gold salt. To do that, 1.5 mM HAuCl4 solution was added to the NGQDs dispersion. To reach the optimal sample, different amounts of gold salt were added to 1 mL of NGQDs.

In the photochemical process, the desired solution was exposed to 16 W ultraviolet lamp radiation for 2 h, to form the NGQDs/Au nanocomposite. The processes that occur between the particles in the solution lead to the creation of bonds between the particles and as a result the formation of nanocomposites. By irradiation of ultraviolet to the solution, HAuCl4 are dissociated into Au3+, H+ and Cl− and simultaneously NGQDs become excited. Because of more positive reduction potential of Au3+ ions compared to NGQDs, a suitable situation is provided for the formation of nanocomposite. The last step would be the aggregation and self-assembly via hydrogen bonding aurophilicity interactions. These two processes lead to the formation of a nanocomposite of NGQDs and gold nanoparticles. The synthesis process is shown in Fig. 1 .

Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) and Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) analysis were performed by a MIRA3 TESCAN instrument. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded by a Philips X'pert instrument with Cu kα radiation. Raman spectra was recorded by a Takram P50C0R10 Raman spectrometer with a 532 nm excitation source. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken by Veeco Autoprobe CP research microscope and CM30 300 kV instrument respectively. UV–Vis spectra were collected by a PerkinElmer Lambda 25 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) emission was also recorded by using a PL spectrometer (Gilden photonics).

The TEM and FESEM images of synthesized NGQD/Au nanocomposite are shown in Fig. 2 . As can be seen from FESEM image, most of the nanoparticles have a size between 50 and 120 nm. The average size of the nanoparticles is around 100 nm. The FESEM image also shows the relatively uniform distribution of the nanoparticles.

In the TEM image, a nanocomposite can be seen separately. The gold nanoparticle with a diameter of 60 nm in the center and the NGQDs nanoparticles (fainter circles) are clearly recognizable around it.

The X-ray diffraction pattern of NGQDs/Au nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 3 a. There is a broad peak around 26–30°, which indicates the graphitic peak of NGQDs in the nanocomposite structure. There are two other peaks around 44° and 49°, which are corresponded to the (111) and (200) planes of gold nanoparticles. The X-ray diffraction plot shows that the new morphology is well formed by placing gold nanoparticles and NGQDs next to each other.

As is shown from the Raman spectrum in Fig. 3b, one peak is at 1401 that is related to incomplete sp2 and sp3 bonds of carbon (D) and another one is at 1581, that is related to crystalline and regular bonds (G).

EDX Analysis was performed to determine the elemental composition of prepared NGQDs/Au nanocomposites as is shown in Fig. 3c. Weight percent of elements indicates 16.08 % Carbon, 13.56 % Nitrogen, 46.43 % Oxygen and 23.93 % gold in the sample that confirms the successful synthesis of NGQDs/Au nanoparticles as it was expected.

As can be seen in Fig. 4 , the absorption spectrum of NGQDs/Au nanocomposites has a broad absorption peak at the wavelength of 572 nm, which is due to the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) of gold nanoparticles. The peaks related to carbon-carbon and carbon-oxygen bonds are also seen at 224 and 349 nm, which, along with the peak at 572 nm, confirm the formation of the nanocomposite structure.

The peak, which is created at 572 nm, can have a variable position depending on the factors such as the shape and the size of gold nanoparticles. Gold nanoparticles concentrate the energy of the photons irradiated to the material by its surface plasmon resonance. Also, the surface plasmon resonance level could have a significant role in recombination of electron-holes, which is explained in the next part.

Fig. 5 a and b show the photoluminescent spectrum of NGQDs and NGQDs/Au nanocomposite at the excitation wavelength of 380 and 460 nm, respectively. As can be seen in Fig. 5, gold nanoparticles cause quenching of the emission spectrum of graphene quantum dots. Accumulation of the NGQDs around each gold nanoparticles leads to a decrease in the distance between them. when this distance become less than a critical limit, it leads to the quenching in luminescence spectrum [29]. This quenching means that the electron-holes recombination decreases. With the accumulation of quantum dots at very small distances from each other around gold nanoparticles, electron clouds interfere with each other and the new energy levels will be formed. This process causes the lack of recombination of the formed electron-holes, which lose their energy in the form of heat. Consequently, the number of photons emitted from the material decreases, which is shown in the photoluminescence spectrum (Fig. 5). As a result, in this case, because of the surface plasmon resonance of gold nanoparticles, there would be a large number of electron-holes, which due to the arrangement of quantum dots around the gold nanoparticles, are not capable of recombination.
The quantum yield of NGQDs/Au nanocomposite was determined by using quinine sulfate as a reference (shown in Table 1 ).It was calculated according to Ref. [28]:

φ = φr (I/Ir)(n2/nr2)(Ar/A) (1) where φ is the quantum yield, n is the refractive index (1.33 for water), I is the measured integrated emission intensity, and A is the optical density. The subscript “r” refers to the reference of known quantum yield. The PL quantum yield was measured by using quinine sulfate as a reference is 0.8 %, which indicates the low number of photons produced by electron-hole recombination (output) compared to the photons irradiated to the material (input). This happens because of the low rate of recombination of electron-holes produced by the material, as a result of which the electron-holes can be used in the photocatalytic process.

The performance of NGQDs/Au nanocomposite in the photodegradation of bilirubin was investigated by examining the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of bilirubin in the presence of nanocomposite. The decrease in the intensity of the peak located at 465 nm in the absorption spectrum of bilirubin can be as an index to measure the degradation performance of NGQDs/Au nanocomposite. The percentage of photodegradation is calculated by the following formula [30]:
(2)
Degradation
% =
C 0
‐
C t
C 0
× 100
Where C0 and Ct are the concentration of bilirubin at time 0 and t, respectively. After adding the nanocomposite to bilirubin solution, the solution was kept in a dark place at room temperature for 60 min. In the dark mode, until the 40th minute, the process of photodegradation was increasing, and after that no change was observed in the intensity of the degradation. The amount of bilirubin degradation by nanocomposite was 21.7 % in the dark mode. Then, the sample was exposed to green light irradiation with the intensity of 5 mW/cm2 and as high as 74.7 % of bilirubin was degraded after 100 min irradiation, which is 5 times more than blue light used in clinical phototherapy without any photocatalyst [24,26]. As a result, 96.4 % of bilirubin was totally degraded that is shown in Fig. 6 . Degradation percent in the dark mode and in the case of green light irradiation is also illustrated in Fig. 7 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for photodegradation of bilirubin under green light irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: The possible mechanism for the photocatalytic process is shown in Fig. 8 . By irradiation of green light, the photo-excited electrons transfer from the LUMO band of bilirubin to the LUMO band of NGQDs, and the holes transfer from HOMO band of NGQDs to HOMO band of bilirubin. In this process, trapping of bilirubin electrons could results in the degradation of its structure. Another process that could happen is reaction of photo-excited electrons and holes with dissolved oxygen and hydroxyl ions in the solution to produce superoxide and hydroxyl radicals, respectively. The reaction of these active radicals with bilirubin molecule is another possible mechanism for the photodegradation process. Au nanoparticles also play a role in the accumulation of quantum dot particles at a very close distance to each other. The interference of electron clouds of particles in these very close distances leads to creation of additional levels in the bandgap and consequently prevention of electron-hole recombination. As a result, unrecombined electrons and holes would participate in the process of creating active radicals. Moreover, the SPR electrons of Au nanoparticles also move toward the LUMO band of NGQDs and participate in the creation of active radicals [29].

In conclusion, a nanocomposite of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots and Au nanoparticles was synthesized by hydrothermal method followed by a photochemical route. The results revealed the potential use of the synthesized nanocomposite as phototherapeutic agents. The NGQDs/Au nanocomposite was utilized as an efficient photocatalyst for photocatalytic degradation of bilirubin under green light irradiation. The prepared NGQDs/Au nanocomposite degraded nearly 96.4 % of bilirubin after 40 min dark condition and 100 min green light irradiation. The high-performance photoactivity of the nanocomposite under green light irradiation could be ascribed to the synergic effect in the nanocomposite, which increases green light harvesting and suppression of charge carriers recombination. Removal efficiency of bilirubin in presence of NGQDs/Au photocatalyst under the green light irradiation was obviously higher than blue light used in clinical phototherapy without any photocatalyst. This research gives new insight into a cheap, instant, and noninvasive photocatalyst-assisted therapeutic protocol for bilirubin removal and demonstrates the great potential of GQDs-based nanocomposite photocatalysts in clinical and therapeutic applications.

Mohamad Taghi Dejpasand: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Saeed Rahemi Ardekani: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Asma Sakhaei Ardekani: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation. Esmaiel Saievar-Iranizad: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Farzad Soleymani: Writing – review & editing, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Research Council of Tarbiat Modares University for financial supports.

Rep. 7(1) (2017) 44338. https://doi.org/10.1038/srep44338.
